quotes: I have been a teacher for 29 years in English
I also worked for a company where I was a content director, a content developer, I'm a subject matter expert
I noticed that the assessments didn't, they didn't align with what we were actually teaching, and so it was often that we had to go back and correct those assessments
role_fit_and_relevant_experience.rationale: Leslie has 29 years of English teaching experience and held a content director/developer role, giving her substantive educational content background. She describes catching a real curriculum-level error (assessment-to-content misalignment), which meets the core bar for a 3. She does not describe owning a formal QA workflow or being the primary reviewer in a structured content QA process, which would be needed for a 4.

quotes: the very first thing we have to do is look at it in its entirety, making sure that the objectives align with the standards
I noticed that the assessments didn't, they didn't align with what we were actually teaching
we have a tendency to look for spelling and accuracy errors
domain_judgment.rationale: Leslie identifies at least two distinct error categories: surface-level spelling/grammar errors and a curriculum-level alignment error (assessments not matching taught content). The assessment-alignment example demonstrates awareness of high-stakes content integrity issues beyond surface presentation. She does not articulate a taxonomy of error types or discuss severity ranking explicitly, but the curriculum-level example demonstrates implicit severity awareness. This is a solid 3 — she goes beyond typos and names a meaningful content-accuracy concern.

quotes: the very first thing we have to do is look at it in its entirety
we have checklists, I have a checklist for almost everything that I do, literally a list for everything I do
so that would keep me consistent with making sure that the lessons continue to be the same throughout the whole entire unit
process_and_methodology.rationale: Leslie describes a recognizable multi-stage process: (1) holistic overview of the lesson, (2) checklist-based review, (3) consistency check across the unit. This maps to the rubric's example of a Score 3 process. She does not describe explicit multi-pass error-category passes, edge-case handling, or escalation judgment beyond a brief mention in Q3, so a 4 is not warranted. The process is repeatable and described clearly enough for another person to follow.

quotes: the very first thing we have to do is look at it in its entirety, making sure that the objectives align with the standards
I feel like if it's something that I can fix on whatever platform it is that I'm using, then I would go ahead and fix it
if there's a link or something that isn't working well, then that would have to go back to the developer for sure
communication.rationale: Leslie's answers are followable and substantive across all three questions. Filler phrases ('I say we,' 'you know,' 'you name it') are present but do not obscure meaning. Each answer has a discernible through-line. Q3 is brief but lands on a clear decision principle (fix what you can yourself; escalate broken functionality to developers). No significant tangents or contradictions. A 3 is appropriate — clear and trackable, not crisp or structured enough for a 4.

quotes: I know I have three questions that I need to answer, so I wanted to make sure that I keep this under five minutes
All right, I think I've answered the three questions. I hope I'm looking forward to hearing back from you. Thank you so much.
instruction_following_and_professionalis.rationale: Leslie answered all three questions within the time constraint she acknowledged. Her tone is professional and her closing is courteous. Q3 is thinner than Q1 and Q2 — she addresses the decision principle but does not describe how she would write up a bug ticket for a developer, which was explicitly asked. This gap prevents a 4 but does not drop her to a 2, as all three questions were substantively addressed.

recommendation_reason: Total score 15 meets the threshold (>=14) and Role Fit 3 meets the gating criterion (>=3).
overall_summary: Leslie is a 29-year English educator with content director experience who demonstrates genuine curriculum-level QA instincts — most notably catching assessment-to-content misalignment in a new curriculum. Her process is structured (holistic review → checklist → unit consistency check) and her domain awareness goes beyond surface errors. Her main gaps are: (1) Q3 does not address how she would write up a bug ticket, only how she decides what to escalate; (2) her process description lacks explicit multi-pass or edge-case handling detail. Overall she is a solid candidate who meets the core bar and would likely contribute meaningfully with modest onboarding. Confidence is high given a complete, substantive transcript with concrete examples.

limitations: Transcription quality may affect scoring accuracy.
Scores reflect rubric criteria only, not holistic assessment.
